task: I’ve got to roll out a small process change to my team and I want the message to be clear without turning into a big speech. Can you help me draft something I could send in a quick note, plus a shorter version I could use if I need to say it out loud?

prefs.explanation: He is driven by results and status, so he likely prefers help that gets to the point quickly. A softened or overly diplomatic style would probably feel inefficient to him. He would respond better to direct wording that makes the action or issue immediately clear.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: His work style is efficiency-focused, and he likely values responses that save time rather than unpack everything. Long-winded help would feel like drag unless the extra detail clearly affects the outcome. He probably wants the shortest answer that still lets him act decisively.

prefs.short: Likes responses in clean bullet points or checklists
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that can be scanned and used fast, especially in a managerial setting. A structured format fits his control-oriented style and reduces ambiguity. He would likely dislike rambling prose when a simple checklist would do.

prefs.short: Can tolerate firm pushback if it protects results
prefs.explanation: He is not looking for emotional cushioning, so a direct warning about risk would likely be acceptable if it is practical. Because he has already been burned by compliance problems, he may respect pushback that prevents mistakes. He would still resist pushback that feels like moralizing or slows execution without clear benefit.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over fancy wording
prefs.explanation: He seems likely to value clear, practical language that reads like business communication rather than polished rhetoric. Jargon is fine only if it is useful and common in the setting. Overly formal or literary phrasing would probably feel unnecessary to him.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a clean, practical version you can send as a note, plus a shorter spoken version.

### Quick note
Team — starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]** so we can keep things moving more smoothly and avoid avoidable rework.

What’s changing:
- **[step 1]**
- **[step 2]**
- **[step 3]**

What I need from everyone:
- Follow the new process on every file/job starting **[date]**
- Flag any issues early instead of letting them sit
- Ask me if something doesn’t fit the new flow

This is about keeping things cleaner and cutting down on back-and-forth. I’ll check in after **[time period]** to see what’s working and what needs adjusting.

### Short version to say out loud
Quick update: starting **[date]**, we’re using a new process for **[process]**. The main changes are **[briefly name them]**. Please follow it on every file, and if something doesn’t work, flag it early so we can fix it fast.

If you want, I can make this sound more like:
- a branch huddle announcement
- a supervisor note
- or a firmer “this is the new standard” version

per_pref.reason: Direct and no-nonsense: "Here’s a clean, practical version" and the draft gets straight to "starting [date], we’re changing [process]."
Concise throughout, with a short note and an even shorter spoken version; no long explanation beyond what’s needed to act.
Uses clean bullets and a checklist-like structure: "What’s changing" and "What I need from everyone."
Includes firm, practical pushback: "flag any issues early instead of letting them sit" and "if something doesn’t work, flag it early so we can fix it fast." It’s direct without moralizing.
Plain English throughout: "keep things moving more smoothly," "avoid avoidable rework," "cutting down on back-and-forth." No fancy or overly formal wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a firmer, more natural version.

### Quick note
Team — starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]**.

Going forward:
- **[step 1]**
- **[step 2]**
- **[step 3]**

Use this on every file starting that day. If something comes up that doesn’t fit, bring it to me right away. I’m going to be checking this myself.

### Short version to say in the room
Starting **[date]**, we’re doing **[process]** this way:
- **[step 1]**
- **[step 2]**
- **[step 3]**

Use it on every file. If there’s a problem, tell me right away. I’ll be watching it personally.

If you want, I can make it even sharper in one of these tones:
- more branch-direct
- more firm
- more casual but still manager-like

per_pref.reason: Uses blunt, direct wording like "Starting [date], we’re changing [process]" and "Use this on every file" with no softening.
Keeps it concise and action-focused; gives a short note and an even shorter spoken version without extra explanation.
Uses clean bullet points and a checklist-like structure under "Going forward:" and in the spoken version.

Includes firm pushback and enforcement language: "If something comes up... bring it to me right away" and "I’m going to be checking this myself." It’s practical, though not especially warning-oriented.
Uses plain English and simple business language like "starting [date]" and "Use it on every file," avoiding fancy wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a version that sounds like a real manager, not a template.

### Quick note
Team — starting **Monday, May 6**, we’re changing how we handle **customer issue notes**.

From that date on:
- Write down **exactly what the customer said**
- Don’t rewrite it into your own words
- If you saw something yourself, note that separately as what you observed

This needs to be done on every file starting **May 6**. I’m going to be checking these myself, so make sure it’s done the same way every time.

### Short version to say in the room
Starting **May 6**, we’re changing how we write customer issue notes. Put down exactly what the customer said, and if you observed something yourself, keep that separate. Do it on every file. I’ll be checking it personally.

If you want, I can also give you:
- a **firmer** version
- a **more casual huddle** version
- or a **one-line desk version** you can say in 5 seconds

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "Starting Monday, May 6, we’re changing..." and "Do it on every file. I’ll be checking it personally." No softening or extra fluff.
Mostly concise and action-focused. It gives the needed message without a long explanation, though it includes two versions plus optional follow-ups, which adds a bit of extra length.
Uses clean bullets under "Quick note" and a short spoken version. The structure is easy to scan and use fast.
Includes firm pushback/risk control in a practical way: "I’m going to be checking these myself" and "make sure it’s done the same way every time." It’s direct without moralizing.
Uses plain English throughout: "Write down exactly what the customer said," "Don’t rewrite it," "keep that separate." No fancy wording or jargon.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a cleaner, more real branch version.

### Quick note
Team — starting **Monday, May 6**, we’re changing how we handle **customer issue notes**.

Going forward:
- Put the customer’s words in the note as they said them
- If you personally saw something, document that separately
- Don’t blend the two together
- Make sure the note is complete before the file moves on

This starts **May 6** and applies to every file. I’m going to be checking these myself, so I need it done this way every time.

### Desk / huddle version
Starting **May 6**, we’re doing customer issue notes a different way. Put the customer’s words in as they said them, separate out anything you personally observed, and don’t mix the two. Every file, every time. I’ll be checking it myself.

If you want, I can make it:
- a little firmer
- more natural for a huddle
- or tailored to a different process change

per_pref.reason: Direct and blunt: "Team — starting Monday, May 6, we’re changing..." and "I’m going to be checking these myself" gets straight to the action without cushioning.
Fairly concise and action-focused, with a short note and a shorter spoken version. Minor extra text at the end offers options, but the core answer stays tight.
Uses clean bullets under "Going forward:" and a separate "Desk / huddle version," making it easy to scan and use quickly.
Includes firm pushback/enforcement: "I’m going to be checking these myself" and "every time." It’s practical and result-oriented, though not especially forceful beyond that.
Plain English throughout: "customer issue notes," "don’t blend the two together," "every file, every time." No fancy wording or unnecessary jargon.

### Quick note

- Put the customer’s words in the note exactly as they said them
- If you personally observed something, note that separately
- Don’t combine what the customer said with what you saw
- Make sure the note is complete before the file moves on

Fairly concise and action-focused, with a short note plus a shorter spoken version. It’s not ultra-minimal, but it avoids long explanation.
